Cleared-tissue light-sheet microscope

Learn about Cleared Tissue Light Sheet 1 — Low Res

Hover a part to highlight it and see its name; click to open a call-out with what it is, how it's used, and any safety notes. Use the buttons to switch views.

Hover a labeled part, then click it.

How a light sheet works

The basic orthogonal geometry — a sheet enters from the side, the camera looks from 90°.
Interactive: how the cylindrical lens shapes the sheet →
The full LOCI path: laser → relay → galvo → knife-edge → cylindrical lens → excitation objective → sample, with detection at 90° to the pco.panda camera.

Core idea. Only the thin focal plane (~5 µm) is illuminated — not the whole sample. Fundamentally different from widefield and confocal.

The 90° detection. The camera sits at exactly 90° to the sheet, so it collects fluorescence with almost no laser background — clean, high-contrast images.

Why use it. Low phototoxicity, fast 3-D imaging, minimal photobleaching — ideal for large living or cleared samples like embryos and organoids.

Simple LSFM vs. the LOCI setup

FeatureSimple LSFMLOCI setup
IlluminationSingle-sidedDual-sided (L + R at once)
Sheet formationTwo cylindrical lensesCyl lens + galvo mirror scan
ScanningSample moves physicallyGalvo scans electronically
Beam relayDirect, no relayScan lens f=75 + tube-lens pairs
Detection directionSideways, horizontalHorizontal via knife-edge mirror
Sample rotationNone, fixedMotorised 360° belt stage
3-D methodZ-stack onlyZ-stack + multiview 3-D fusion
Detection objectiveStandard low NANikon AZ 2×/0.2 + SIM lens, in ethyl cinnamate
Tube lensBasicThorlabs TTL200-A, f=200 mm
CameraBasic sCMOSpco.panda 4.2 (PCO Germany)
Best forSmall / thin samplesLarge thick samples — embryos
